summary: DHH highlights a SunSpot.net article contrasting Mac OS X's security posture with Windows XP during a major virus outbreak. While Windows users were getting knocked offline by the Blaster worm, Mac users continued working unaffected. He compares the default attack surface of both operating systems, finding OS X exposes only one port by default versus five for Windows XP. He corrects the original article's claim that OS X has its firewall enabled by default.
key_points: Mac OS X had zero viruses in the wild while Windows suffered repeated major outbreaks like Blaster
Default OS X installations expose only 1 open port compared to 5 for Windows XP, dramatically reducing attack surface
Windows XP's open port 135 was the exact entry point for the Blaster worm
Neither OS had its built-in firewall enabled by default, correcting the original article's claim about OS X
money_quotes: Macintosh users did what they usually do during a computer virus outbreak — they continued working.
My freshly installed version of OS X only has a single open port.
My default Windows XP installation is another matter. Without starting any programs on my own accord, there's five open ports.
The first is of course where Blaster makes its entry.
Neither operating system has the build-in firewall turned on by default.
key_insight: Mac OS X's minimal default attack surface — one open port versus Windows XP's five — illustrated a fundamental design philosophy difference that left Mac users unaffected by the virus outbreaks plaguing the Windows world.
